How sourdough hydration changes the crumb

Hydration — the ratio of water to flour by weight — is the single biggest lever in sourdough. Pulling it from 65 percent up to 80 percent transforms a tight sandwich-style crumb into the open, glossy, irregular structure most home bakers chase.

What happens at lower hydration

At 65 percent the dough is firm and easy to shape. The crumb stays tight and even, and the loaf rises in a predictable, rounded dome. It slices cleanly the next day, which is why this hydration is the workhorse for sandwich loaves and pan breads.

What happens at higher hydration

At 78 to 82 percent the dough is wet and slack. Gluten development takes longer and the dough wants to spread, so you compensate with stretches and folds during bulk fermentation. The reward is dramatic oven spring and a wide-open crumb with shiny, gelatinized walls.

Where to start

Most bakers find 75 percent hydration a sweet spot — open enough to look impressive, dry enough to shape without panic. From there move up or down 2 percent at a time and observe how the dough behaves at each step.
